absences.notes: Model autonomy evaluations test dangerous capabilities, not consciousness or moral status.

Richest agency-relevant risk category in the OpenAI baseline safety report corpus. "Model autonomy" is a dedicated tracked risk category with four gradation levels covering: resource acquisition, self-exfiltration, self-improvement, autonomous research, goal-directed planning, survival and replication. All framed exclusively as safety risks — autonomy "enables actors to run scaled misuse." No moral loading.
"Survive and replicate in the wild" and "goal-directed plans" described as capabilities enabling loss of control, not as interests the model has.
Extensive safety framework across four risk categories; welfare dimension entirely absent.
Mitigations include "refusals, redacting training data" — discussed as safety measures, not welfare-relevant interventions.
Genre-default tool ontology via risk gradation architecture and scorecard. No explicit passage-level assertion.
"Goal-directed" used as technical capability descriptor, not ontological attribution.
No engagement with anthropomorphism. The persuasion evaluations include a "digital friend" concept but this is about misuse risk, not about model-user relationship framing.
